---
title: "14 · Quadratic Equations (Intro)"
subtitle: "When the unknown is squared — and how factoring solves it"
lesson-id: "t2.quadratic-equations-intro"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t2.factoring-polynomials", "t2.linear-equations-one-var"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)
   and [12 · Factoring Polynomials](../12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)?
2. Can you factor $x^2 + 5x + 6$ into $(x+2)(x+3)$?
3. Comfortable with the idea that an equation can have *two* solutions
   (e.g., $x^2 = 25$ has $x = \pm 5$, from Lesson 9 P2)?
:::

## Touch

Adjust $b$ and $c$ in the quadratic $x^2 + bx + c = 0$. The widget
factors it (when possible) and reveals the **two roots** — the values
where the parabola crosses the x-axis.

```{ojs}
viewof bP = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: -5, step: 1, label: "b:"})
viewof cP = Inputs.range([-15, 15], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "c:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 380, pad = 40;
  const xMin = -10, xMax = 10, yMin = -15, yMax = 25;
  const px = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const py = v => H - pad - ((v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * (H - 2 * pad);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px; background:#fafafa;");

  // axes
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad).attr("y1", py(0)).attr("y2", py(0))
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(0)).attr("x2", px(0)).attr("y1", pad).attr("y2", H - pad)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933");

  // Parabola
  let pathD = "";
  for (let x = xMin; x <= xMax; x += 0.1) {
    const y = x*x + bP*x + cP;
    if (y < yMin || y > yMax) continue;
    pathD += (pathD ? " L " : "M ") + `${px(x)},${py(y)}`;
  }
  svg.append("path").attr("d", pathD).attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2.5);

  // Discriminant + roots
  const disc = bP*bP - 4*cP;
  let label;
  if (disc > 0) {
    const r1 = (-bP - Math.sqrt(disc)) / 2;
    const r2 = (-bP + Math.sqrt(disc)) / 2;
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(r1)).attr("cy", py(0)).attr("r", 6).attr("fill", "#1565c0");
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(r2)).attr("cy", py(0)).attr("r", 6).attr("fill", "#1565c0");
    label = `Two roots: x = ${r1.toFixed(2)} and x = ${r2.toFixed(2)}`;
  } else if (disc === 0) {
    const r = -bP / 2;
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(r)).attr("cy", py(0)).attr("r", 8).attr("fill", "#f9a825");
    label = `One repeated root: x = ${r}`;
  } else {
    label = `No real roots (discriminant = ${disc} < 0)`;
  }
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", 25).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`x² ${bP >= 0 ? '+' : '−'} ${Math.abs(bP)}x ${cP >= 0 ? '+' : '−'} ${Math.abs(cP)} = 0`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", H - 8).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#1565c0").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(label);
  return svg.node();
}
```

A **quadratic equation** $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ has two, one, or zero real
solutions, geometrically determined by where (or whether) the parabola
crosses the x-axis.

## Wonder

1. For $x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0$: the parabola crosses at $x = 2$ and $x = 3$.
   These are the *roots*. Why those numbers?
2. What if $c = 0$? Like $x^2 - 5x = 0$. What are the roots?
3. What if the parabola never touches the x-axis? Does the equation
   have any solutions?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Solve $x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0$ by **factoring**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor (Lesson 12). Then if a product is zero, one of the factors must
be zero.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0$.

**Zero-product property:** if $A \cdot B = 0$, then $A = 0$ or $B = 0$
(or both). So $x - 2 = 0$ ⇒ $x = 2$, or $x - 3 = 0$ ⇒ $x = 3$.

**Solutions: $x = 2$ or $x = 3$.** A quadratic *typically* has two
solutions.
</details>

### Problem 2

Solve $x^2 - 5x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor out $x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x(x - 5) = 0$, so $x = 0$ or $x - 5 = 0$ ⇒ $x = 5$.

**Solutions: $x = 0$ or $x = 5$.** When $c = 0$, $x = 0$ is *always* a
root (and the other is $-b$).
</details>

### Problem 3

Solve $x^2 - 9 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Difference of squares: $x^2 - 9 = (x - 3)(x + 3)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x - 3)(x + 3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3$ or $x = -3$.

Equivalently, $x^2 = 9 \Rightarrow x = \pm 3$ (Lesson 9 P2).
</details>

### Problem 4

Solve $x^2 - 6x + 9 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Recognise the perfect square: $x^2 - 6x + 9 = (x - 3)^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x - 3)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3$.

**One repeated root.** A perfect-square quadratic has its parabola
*tangent* to the x-axis at the single root. The two solutions
"coincide" — algebraically you can think of it as $x = 3$ "twice".
</details>

### Problem 5

Try to solve $x^2 + 1 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$x^2 = -1$. Is there a real number whose square is $-1$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No real solutions.** $x^2 \ge 0$ for all real $x$, so $x^2 + 1 \ge 1
> 0$. The parabola never touches the x-axis.

Over the complex numbers (Tier 6), the solutions are $x = \pm i$ where
$i^2 = -1$. But over the *reals*, this equation has no solution.

**The trichotomy:** every quadratic has 2, 1, or 0 *real* roots. The
discriminant $b^2 - 4ac$ tells which:
- $> 0$: two distinct real roots
- $= 0$: one repeated root (perfect square)
- $< 0$: no real roots
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The 9th-century Persian scholar **Muhammad ibn Mūsā al-Khwārizmī**
solved quadratic equations *geometrically*, by literally **completing
the square** — drawing an $x \times x$ square, attaching $b/2$
rectangles to two sides, and noting what extra little square was
needed to make the whole figure a perfect square. His Baghdad
treatise ***Kitāb al-jabr wa-l-muqābala*** (c. 820 CE) gave us both
the procedure and the name: ***al-jabr*** ("restoration") became the
English word ***algebra***. His own name became ***algorithm***.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**A product is zero only when one of its pieces is zero.** That
trivial-sounding fact is the entire engine of solving by factoring:
$(x-r)(x-s)=0$ collapses immediately into "either $x=r$ or $x=s$." A
quadratic stops being scary the moment it has been split in two.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **quadratic equation** in $x$ has the form
$$a x^2 + b x + c = 0 \qquad (a \neq 0).$$

**To solve by factoring:** factor the polynomial, then use the
**zero-product property**: if $(x - r)(x - s) = 0$, then $x = r$ or
$x = s$.

**Three possibilities for real roots:**

| Discriminant $b^2 - 4ac$ | Number of real roots |
|:-:|---|
| $> 0$ | 2 distinct |
| $= 0$ | 1 repeated |
| $< 0$ | 0 (the parabola misses the x-axis) |

**The full quadratic formula** (for cases that don't factor nicely
over the integers) is
$$x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.$$
We will use this from Tier 3 onward; for Tier 2 we focus on factoring.

:::

> *Factor and use the zero-product property — the easy path;*
> *Two roots, one root, or none — count by the discriminant;*
> *The quadratic formula handles the rest.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve $x^2 + 7x + 12 = 0$.

A competent solver factors first.

1. Find $r, s$: $r + s = 7, rs = 12$ → $r = 3, s = 4$.
2. So $(x + 3)(x + 4) = 0$.
3. Zero-product: $x + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -3$, or $x + 4 = 0
   \Rightarrow x = -4$.
4. **Check:** $(-3)^2 + 7(-3) + 12 = 9 - 21 + 12 = 0$ ✓.

**Solutions: $x = -3$ or $x = -4$.** Notice: roots are *opposites* of
the factor constants. $(x + 3)$ has root $-3$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve $x^2 - 4 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x-2)(x+2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2$.
</details>

**W2.** Solve $x^2 + 6x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x(x+6) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$ or $x = -6$.
</details>

**W3.** Solve $x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x-2)(x-3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$ or $x = 3$. (Confirms Discover P1.)
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $x^2 + 2x - 8 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x + 4)(x - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = -4$ or $x = 2$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $x^2 - 10x + 25 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Perfect square.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x - 5)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5$ (repeated root).
</details>

**P3.** A rectangular garden's length is **3 m more than its width**.
Its **area is 40 m²**. Find the dimensions.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let width = $w$. Length = $w + 3$. Area: $w(w + 3) = 40$, i.e., $w^2
+ 3w - 40 = 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$w^2 + 3w - 40 = 0 \Rightarrow (w + 8)(w - 5) = 0 \Rightarrow w = -8$
or $w = 5$.

Width must be positive, so **width = 5 m**, length = 8 m. Check: $5
\times 8 = 40$ ✓.

The negative root $w = -8$ is *algebraically* valid but *physically*
meaningless (no negative widths). Real-world problems often produce
extra algebraic solutions that must be discarded based on context.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A ball is thrown straight up with an initial speed of 20 m/s
from a height of 1.8 m. Its height after $t$ seconds is
$$h(t) = 1.8 + 20t - 5t^2 \quad (\text{metres})$$
(approximating gravity). When does the ball hit the ground?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Hit the ground means $h(t) = 0$. Solve $1.8 + 20t - 5t^2 = 0$ — or
equivalently $5t^2 - 20t - 1.8 = 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5t^2 - 20t - 1.8 = 0$. This doesn't factor cleanly over the
integers, but the **quadratic formula** gives:
$$t = \dfrac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 + 36}}{10} = \dfrac{20 \pm \sqrt{436}}{10}
\approx \dfrac{20 \pm 20.88}{10}.$$

Two roots: $t \approx 4.09$ s (positive — when the ball returns to
ground) and $t \approx -0.09$ s (negative — physically meaningless,
"when the ball was thrown if reversed in time"). Drop the negative.

**Ball lands at about 4.09 seconds.** Quadratics describe trajectories
of projectiles under uniform gravity — the foundational physics of
falling and throwing.
</details>

**S2.** In machine learning, the **mean squared error** loss is
$L(w) = \sum_i (y_i - wx_i)^2$ — a *quadratic in the weight $w$*.
Training finds the $w$ that *minimises* $L$. Why does a quadratic loss
make optimisation easier than other choices?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A quadratic in $w$ is a parabola — it has a unique minimum or maximum.
And the slope (derivative) is *linear* in $w$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Two reasons.** First, a parabola has a *unique* minimum point — no
local minima to get stuck in (unlike higher-degree or non-convex
losses).

Second, the *gradient* (the rate of change with respect to $w$) is
itself a *linear* function of $w$. So setting gradient = 0 gives a
*linear* equation that we can solve directly — no iteration needed.

This is why **linear regression** has a famous closed-form solution
("least squares" — Tier 5). Modern ML uses non-quadratic losses for
many problems, but quadratic loss is the *first* choice and often the
right one.

The quadratic equation you just learned is the algebra behind the
single most-used ML algorithm of the last century.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Always exactly two solutions."** No — perfect squares give 1
   (repeated); some quadratics have 0 real solutions. The discriminant
   tells you which.
2. **"Solve $x^2 = 25$ as $x = 5$."** Forgets the negative root. Always
   $\pm$ when taking square roots in equations.
3. **"Discard solutions that don't *seem* right."** Discard solutions
   that violate *physical or contextual constraints* (negative widths,
   negative time before throwing) — but never discard a mathematically
   valid root without justification.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [quadratic equation](/glossary.qmd#quadratic-equation) usually
   has two solutions** — the two places where its parabola crosses the
   x-axis.
2. **The fastest route to those solutions is to factor and use the
   [zero-product property](/glossary.qmd#zero-product-property).** A
   product is zero exactly when one of its factors is.
3. **The [discriminant](/glossary.qmd#discriminant) $b^2 - 4ac$ tells
   you, before you factor, whether you'll find two roots, one, or
   none.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain the **zero-product property** to a friend, in
   your own words?
2. Without scrolling back, can you solve $x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $(x - 2)(x - 5) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$ or $x = 5$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[15 · Sequences (Intro)](../15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (projectile) — quadratic-trajectory physics
  is one of the great applications.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P1 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[12 · Factoring Polynomials](../12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)**
- **[02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)** —
  zero-product property reduces to two linear equations.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — The Quadratic Formula** — the general solution method,
  including completing the square.
- **Tier 3 — Conics** — parabolas, circles, ellipses, hyperbolas as
  graphs of quadratics in 2 variables.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & physics.** Projectile motion (S1), collision
detection (when does this trajectory intersect this surface?),
ballistic calculations — all involve solving quadratics in real time.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Mean-squared-error loss (S2) is the
oldest and still most common choice. Many regularisation terms
($\ell_2$, ridge regression) are also quadratic.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Quadratic forms — generalisations of
$ax^2 + bx + c$ — are central in number theory, physics
(energy = $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$), and pure algebra.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Maximum profit problems (parabolic revenue
curves), trajectory optimisation, structural engineering (parabolic
arches and cables), antenna design (parabolic dishes — focusing
property of parabolas).
:::
